title: Repairing a rotted fascia board
subtitle: Cut out and splice a new section into a partially rotted fascia
excerpt: How to repair a timber fascia board where rot has affected part of its length: cutting out the failed section, treating the remaining sound timber, fitting a splice, finishing flush, and painting for weather protection.

glossaryTerms.definition: The horizontal timber board fixed to the rafter ends at the eaves of a roof. It carries the gutter bracket and forms the visible face of the eaves. Softwood fascias on houses built before the 1990s are frequently painted and are susceptible to rot where the paint film is broken by gutter overflows or the end grain at junctions.

content.text: can be repaired by cutting out the rotted section and splicing in a new length. Full replacement is the professional approach on a heavily affected board. A clean splice lasts 10 to 15 years with the right treatment and finish.

Work at height from a ladder requires a properly footed ladder, a second person to foot the ladder, and the discipline to not over-reach. All working-at-height safety applies. If the fascia is above the first-floor eaves level, scaffolding is the appropriate platform.
Step 1, assess the rot and remove the gutter

Probe the fascia with a bradawl or penknife blade to find the full extent of the rot: the soft spongy area is wider than the visible damage. Remove the gutter bracket or brackets in the affected section and support the gutter temporarily. Cut the gutter joint open if needed; a leaking gutter joint above the fascia rot is often the cause of the damage.
Step 2, cut out the failed section

Mark two cut lines at least 150 mm into the sound timber on each side of the rot. Cut vertically with a tenon saw or a jigsaw. Remove the cut section. The cut ends should bear on a rafter end; if they fall between rafter ends, add a nogging between the nearest two rafters to provide a bearing for the splice.

Brush two coats of preservative timber treatment into the cut ends and the back face of the remaining fascia. Allow to dry fully. Apply a thick primer coat to all new cut surfaces, paying particular attention to the end grain, which is the primary route for water entry.

Cut the new section of fascia to length to match the profile exactly. Treat all faces as above. Fix with 50 mm galvanised screws into the rafter ends, and use countersunk screws at the joints with the existing fascia. The joint at each end should fall on a rafter end. Fill screw holes and the joint line with a flexible exterior filler, sand flush, and prime immediately.
Step 5, finish and re-fix the gutter

Apply two full coats of exterior gloss paint, including the top edge and the back face where accessible. Re-fix the gutter brackets; replace any suspect gutter joint seals while the gutter is off. A silicone-sealed gutter joint above the repair is cheaper than a repeat job.

attrs.symptom: Splice joint cracks within the first season
attrs.cause: Joint fell in a span without a rafter bearing, or the flexible filler was not used at the joint line.
attrs.fix: Add a nogging behind the joint and re-seal the joint line with flexible exterior filler.
